speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting shiva after my brother. And my mother was there. And as soon the shiva was over, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what we found out later, my mother went to her father on-- to pray on December 30 because my uncle was running from one side to the other if she did something before she commit suicide. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm sorry. [DRINKS] [SIGHS] Then I was sitting shiva [INAUDIBLE]. And she left three letters. If you want it, I can let you have it to write, you know, to copy. One was to me and my father, who was in Paris at that time. One was to my aunt. And one was to my uncle to take care of me. Excuse me. Hey. And this was a very loving family, as I told you before. I was still in the business because I was allowed still to go to the business. By the way, in the meantime, the business was in German hands. But he was terrible Gestapo, one of the very, very bad people. But somehow, he did let me come that I should-- and he gave me a regular payment like the girl that was with us that was working-- not the Jewish girl-- that was working in the store. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then we seen half a year-- I think it was in '41, I figure, or something like that-- they started to take us into the ghetto. And some people didn't get permission to stay in ghetto. They were sent-- I mean, they had to get out from Krakow. And they were all over villages and so on. But we got one room. We, it means my aunt, uncle, her son, and myself. We could take some furnitures with us at that time. Anyway, we moved through. I remember, it must have-- no, no, wait-- it must have been wintertime, because I remember the walls were completely icy. I never saw anything like it. And this was certain sections that have probably poor houses or whatever. And I was in mourning. And they did let me get out from ghetto still to the store. And Saturdays and Sundays, I was taken to, to clean-- I mean, not only me, but lots of group of women or group of even children, because I was 14 that time. And we had to clean the streets from the snow and so on. And this was still something that we can, we could talk with each other and something like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I met somebody. I went to visit my uncle in the hospital. And there was next to him a young man that was also had surgery. And his friend ask me if he can come and pick me up from the store, take me to ghetto, and back and forth. And he was from Hungary. In the beginning, Hungarian Jews were not persecuted. Then I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schwartz, and the first name is was Milek. I still have a little gift from him, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about your mother's suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted to tell you. My daughter was very upset when she heard that. And my cousin explained-- not explained, but translated from Polish to English-- and she was very upset. I was not. I understood that she couldn't live without her son. And maybe she couldn't face the life without him after-- we always thought that will be-- in one year, in half a year, it will be over. Matter of fact-- no, that's not important. But I understand her. I didn't after two, three days. But the first day after she was on the bed that I ran for my father's medical pouch, and I wanted to take also something. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: To feel better, or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, to just commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the pouch?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- probably morphine and, you know, all kinds of shots and stuff like they give to-- I don't know exactly what it was. I didn't read it. But I knew what it was, that it was something for pain and some things. And must have been poison and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought about doing that, but didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they caught me and [LAUGHS] took the pouch. I mean, my uncle caught me. And then they started to really watch me. They didn't think I would do something like that, but maybe because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw my mother didn't want to live, then I didn't want to live. And that was, that was it. And then my uncle and my aunt and everybody around would explain to me what's what, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they said, look, your mother couldn't live with one child. Because if Daddy would come, he would just be very, very upset that we couldn't live through. And it was very loving couple. And she wouldn't do anything wrong to him. So maybe that's how she felt. I really don't know exactly, because there-- this was not in the letters. In the letters was only goodbye letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the letter to you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I should-- I'm sorry-- [CRYING] that I should wait for my father and everything will be all right. And I should forgive her. And I should not worry about it. But she couldn't live anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that time, we all understand those things. But I was shocked that my daughter didn't understand it, that she, she was so angry at my mother. But later on, we, we were sent to ghetto, as I told you before. And we were able to take certain things that I said to stay. So at that time, also, we had a tenant. One bedroom was his.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a teacher of music. And he also-- he was from Auschwitz, from city of Auschwitz. And he was one of the top musicians that played solo in theaters and so on. And he played with me. I, I am jumping. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- you see, I didn't want to, to play on the piano, you know, to, to exercise. And he said, I tell you what I will do for you. We will take very nice pieces, and I will be your accompanist. And that's how I started to play again, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? It was ?] then he took over the apartment. And he brought his wife from Auschwitz, and so on. And this guy would go near ghetto and would throw food for us, and even in Plaszow. Because from ghetto, I was sent to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto? I was still in mourning. And I was very strict with it. We had a very nice boy who delivered milk to us. He had somebody from the village bring few liters of milk every day about before 6:00. And they would smuggle it. And he fell in love with me. I didn't even know it. [LAUGHS] Only my aunt told him that-- to forget it, I am in mourning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after the mourning was over, he came. He introduced himself. And he said, I know you. I said, where from? [LAUGHS] So he told me. And then he told me to ask my aunt if it's OK to go on a date, which was like walking on the street maybe for an hour or so and talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- we had-- In one house, they were collecting furnitures from Jewish people, Germans. It was an empty building. And this building was guarded by one person, also Jewish, from school. And they had a two-bedroom, two-room apartment in the cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got together, about six couples or five couples. And we decided to-- you know, to talk politics. And they let us do it. And then one day, I brought a tiny little gramophone that I made myself. It was that much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, of course, took some records from the store as long as I could still go there. And we were dancing. But nobody could hear, because it was also against the law.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- this, I, I you know, important to know that to-- for us to live through, even in Auschwitz or in Plaszow, we were singing very, very quiet. You know, nobody should hear except this little group. And this what kept us going, that we will see each other in the evening. And I don't know, there was also a 8:00 or whatever that everybody should be in their own place, you know. This was in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, my uncle told me, you cannot stay here. Because the place where we used to go to work wouldn't let us work there anymore. It was by Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the Krakow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was-- I am already in Plaszow. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Well, I wanted to know just a little bit more about the Krakow ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Krakow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you moved from your house--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we got one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who, who was in that room exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin-- the little cousin-- my aunt and my uncle. And my aunt and uncle, this was sister and brother. So they were both my mother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, it must have been when it was very cold. Because we went with the furnitures and few things into the one room that I mentioned. The wall was ice. And, you know, it-- I had-- we didn't have electricity there, so I putted a candle on. And I stay overnight with my cousin. And the candle showed me that this was ice. We touched it. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't even think you can imagine it. You know? So probably there was steam or something. And it's became ice. When you were brought into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then, the next day, the rest of the crowd came in. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what, what were the rules that the Germans made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. Not much. The first day, everything was, you know, upside down. I think they left the ghetto open for the people to get in within few days. That's what I figured out. So then the ghetto had-- first of all, we all had to go to work at certain time. But I still got a pass to go to the store. So I could get out from the ghetto. And there was no food, no-- excuse me, there was food. My father used to send us into the ghetto white pepper. And this what kept us alive. First of all, the post office would run with the pepper to us, because they were sneezing all over it. [LAUGHTER] And then my oldest aunt knew lots of people in Krakow. And especially, she used to sell it to people where they make ham and stuff like that. Because nobody could get white pepper. And the whole family, we lived on that. This I remember. But I had to go every day except Saturday and Sunday. I had to go to the store, which was good for me. Because at least I was in a different surroundings. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it from where you were living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a good half an hour walk. It was completely different section where I was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans were running the store, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: One German. You see, they got like family. This is yours. Besides that, he was a Gestapo. And he didn't work in the store, only his wife. And sometimes he used to bring her boy. And he knew I was Jewish, so he was very angry at me. This was like Hitler's Jugend. You heard about it. But they did let me go to the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, then one day, I got a telephone from my uncle from ghetto. Don't come in. I said, why? What's happening? They are taking people and sending out. Stay in the store or go to somebody around. So the girl that worked for us took me to her house. Of course, I took off the band, you know, the Star of David. That's what Krakow had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I stayed for the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mittagessen, which is the, the meal. The main meal was at noontime, and then went back to the store. And the uncle said somehow he will find to call me. And then he called me that I can come back for. And this was the first time that they took people away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the intelligence action, what they called that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I've never heard this, intelligent action? No. They took everybody that they could. From the street, people were running in the houses. And I saw lots of blood on the streets when I walked in. And then when it was-- well, then it was the same. And then I couldn't go any more to, to the store. But I got three or four days that I can still stay and then no more. And then all of the sudden, I saw the Gestapo man with another fellow from the same branch, from the same street. And they walked straight into the ghetto. So I walked faster. And I looked at them. And of course, the guy told me-- the Gestapo guy-- the other one was afraid. But the Gestapo guy said, Freda, you coming with us. And he went straight to one of the guys that had something to do, like maybe he was hired as an officer, whatever. And they know each other. And he says, look, you let her alone. And let him alone with the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, how can I do it? But OK, for you I do. And he told me, out you go [LAUGHS] and got the piece of paper. So this was for me, you know, and for the other guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, the second time when they had this, they were-- first they were starting with the hospital. And they putted a big truck under the windows. And they were throwing women that were in-- that were just during the birth of a child. And two child, two children-- I mean two babies fell out of her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And there was screaming and lots of blood and stuff like that. And then they also used to take small children-- like two, three, four-- by the legs and hit it against the buildings. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from our window. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they took people and kept it in one-- not the street, but a [INAUDIBLE], you know, place, one place. And then the car came and took them to the railroad. And then they were going to Auschwitz. And the ghetto started to be smaller and smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- I was supposed to go to Auschwitz with my group. But I got, how do you call it, jaundice? Yeah, so they let me stay for a few days. And then I had to go to Auschwitz. I was staying-- not Auschwitz, Plaszow. How did you know where people were being sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sent? Well, I didn't know. People knew it, you know what I mean, people that probably were going with them and coming back with the train and taking back and forth, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about what was going on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew from my brother that this is a dead end. And I knew that the people had to stand up else. Matter of fact, I had to stand one up in, in Plaszow. It was one man went through, through the gate. And they saw that he had something in his pocket. And it was piece of bread. And they hanged him. This was the one thing I saw. Because we had to look. If somebody would look like that, they would beat you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when it came-- OK, then they were supposed to take my group-- group, I mean the barrack-- to showers, because we didn't have showers yet. OK? Not that they would let us use it. [LAUGHS] But they sent us somewhere where they-- my group, they sent. I didn't go because I had still-- I was still sick. And I-- somehow, I stayed in the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, my friend-- I had in the ghetto another friend that was in there. I was very popular-- [LAUGHS] and then, my friend went to my uncle and says, do something. Don't let her go there. Because he went day before with a group of-- another group. And he said that the group with females, they ask them to get undressed. And the soldiers were just all around. And they were laughing and this and that. I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they wouldn't rape them, because this was really against the war, you know, then they would be shot. That was one thing that was in our favor, that they, they couldn't rape a Jewish girl. OK? If they did or not, then it wasn't publicized, OK? So the women were very upset. The water didn't work. And then they brought them, crushed, back to Plaszow. So my uncle didn't-- so my friend ask my uncle to do anything he can, but not to let me go and go through things like that. So he putted me on a truck that went to ghetto with other things. I don't remember what it was. But they went to a, a bath house. And he told me, as soon you jump out, run and mix within the people in ghetto. And then he told me where to go, the address. I think I told you about it. Did I? And [SIGHS] I was there. There were two chairs. There was no, no place even to lie down. And that one chair was empty. And one was for me. I don't know how could I sleep? I think I stayed around 10 days or something like that. Where were you exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in an-- in one of the houses that had two or three floors, three floors. And on one of the floors, there was a family that would take me in. And this was also-- their daughter worked with me when we had to go out from out from ghetto into the workplace. And they had the hole in the back of the bed. And this was like a little space there, OK? And they putted a carpet or, or something over the hole. And then it was-- the bed was against that. And then another lady walked in, whom I know because we also worked together. So we sat together. Now, one day, my uncle walked in. And he says, that's enough. At 6:00, be ready tomorrow morning. I'm taking you out of the ghetto. And [? Renna ?] will go with you. That's my cousin. I think I showed you the picture there. And that's how we got out. And we went with our aunt. It was maybe an hour of walk from Krakow. She was in a village there, the woman that took us. She was also a maid for my aunt for about 10 or 15 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What village did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I will write it for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what-- when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- oh, why it was. Because my uncle heard it will be judenrein. You know what is judenrein? All the Jews will be taken out. So he was scared. And by the way, when I ran away-- you know, I am jumping. Because I didn't write it, so it comes to my mind this way. There were-- in the same week, there were four or five girls that ran away from Plaszow, one way or the other. And one mother-- there, there was, of course, they were looking still in ghetto for, for them. And one of the girls' mother was taken. And then was another girl taken. They found her. But the mother was right away, the girl-- this daughter of hers, got to Plaszow and to take place of her mother. And they allowed it. And these two girls were hanged. So I couldn't go back to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was when the ghetto was-- it was in '43, has to be in-- had to be in '43, something like beginning of '43. And so this woman that was taking care of us for two, two nights, she said she cannot keep us any longer because her, her husband's brother was crazy. And he might walk around and spill that we are there, spill the news.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we went to the place where we stayed for big money. And then, when the money was gone and that when they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] found the jewelry and everything, then they told my aunt, you'd better get out with both of them. Because we don't want to have you here. You-- we will call the Gestapo to come for you. So we had-- again, we stayed on the street. And from one to the other, somehow, she found everybody, you know, [LAUGHS] but only for one night. And then I told her, you know what, how about if we go on Gentile papers? So she found a place where she could get for us papers. I have them. I, I just don't know where I putted them. But if you need the papers, I will look and send it to you, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: We should break here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
